input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a laboratory incubator used to heat and maintain a target temperature inside an insulated styrofoam box. It consists of a heat‑bed for heating, a fan for air circulation, two internal temperature sensors, one external temperature sensor, and a Raspberry Pi that runs the control software. The plant is a cyber‑physical system (CPS) whose operational domain is temperature regulation for incubation, and its simple yet representative hardware makes it suitable for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides data acquisition from the three temperature sensors, incorporates models of the plant and its environment, runs calibration algorithms to continuously update model parameters, executes simulation‑based predictions of temperature trajectories under current operating conditions, supplies monitoring algorithms that assess safety and optimal performance, and offers optimisation algorithms to adjust controller set‑points. These services target engineers and operators to enhance safety, predictability, and maintenance of the incubator.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a Raspberry Pi as the embedded controller, the open‑source GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) that supplies the hardware description and software source, calibration algorithms that update model parameters, monitoring algorithms that assess safety and optimality, and generic DT components such as data acquisition from sensors, physics‑based models of the thermal plant, and optimisation algorithms. No specific simulation engine or database is named in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT is organized as a unified framework that ingests time‑stamped sensor data from the physical twin, applies calibration algorithms to estimate parameters and update the cyber‑physical system and environment models, then supplies the refreshed models to monitoring algorithms that evaluate safety and optimality. The loop can involve human operators or other automated algorithms to trigger actuation on the physical twin. This centralized data‑model‑algorithm pipeline is illustrated in Figure 1 of the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator DT incorporates two simulation models of the internal temperature. The first model uses two free parameters; the second, more detailed model uses four free parameters to capture thermal inertia of the heat‑bed. Both models are analytical (differential‑equation based) representations of heat transfer and are used together with data collected from the physical incubator during operation. The data consist of temperature measurements over time (collected via the incubator’s onboard sensors) and are stored for calibration of the model parameters. Model calibration is performed by a calibration algorithm that updates the model parameters to minimise the error between simulated and measured temperature. The models are linked to the cyber component (controller software) and the physical component (heat‑bed) forming the DT constellation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The actuation hardware consists of a resistive heatbed that provides the heating power to raise the incubator’s internal temperature. Temperature regulation is performed by a bang‑bang controller implemented on a Raspberry Pi (or equivalent SBC) that switches the heatbed on or off based on the deviation from a setpoint. Control commands are issued remotely via a software layer that communicates with the Raspberry Pi through a RabbitMQ message‑broker server over the incubator’s Wi‑Fi/Bluetooth network. The actuation range is limited to the heatbed’s rated power (unspecified) and the controller enforces safety by preventing overheating, but explicit safety constraints are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Temperature sensing is provided by three DS18S20 high‑precision 1‑Wire digital thermometers: two sensors are mounted inside the incubator to monitor the air temperature, and one sensor is placed outside to measure ambient temperature. Each sensor measures from –55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of ±0.5 °C. The sensors are sampled continuously (exact sampling frequency not specified) and the digital readings are transmitted via the 1‑Wire bus to the Raspberry Pi, which forwards the data over the Wi‑Fi/Bluetooth link to the DT data store.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity was evaluated experimentally by comparing the two temperature simulation models against measured temperature trajectories of the physical incubator. The four‑parameter model demonstrated higher accuracy in reproducing the thermal inertia of the heat‑bed, indicating superior fidelity for dynamic behaviour. Validation consisted of running the incubator under controlled conditions, recording temperature data, and computing prediction errors. Uncertainty sources include sensor noise and simplifications in the heat‑transfer equations. Calibration algorithms were used to reduce parameter uncertainty, and the resulting calibrated model serves as the validated representation within the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Based on Document 1, the Digital Twin continuously runs simulations that reproduce and predict the physical twin’s behaviour under current operating conditions. Calibration algorithms ingest sensor data to update model parameters, and monitoring algorithms evaluate the updated models to detect safety or performance violations. When a violation is identified, the DT generates an alert and presents a recommendation, allowing a human operator or an automated controller to decide on corrective actions for the physical twin. The DT therefore provides predictive insights, fault detection, and optimisation recommendations derived from data‑driven models and algorithms.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical twin as a laboratory incubator with a heatbed, fan, and temperature sensors housed in a styrofoam box. It correctly identifies the Raspberry Pi as the controller and characterizes the system as a simple, representative CPS suitable for digital twinning. Quotes: 'The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed', 'The physical components in the incubator form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi', 'This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS'.

The description comprehensively lists the services provided by the Digital Twin, such as data acquisition, modeling, calibration, simulation, monitoring, and optimization. It aligns perfectly with the definitions and goals outlined in the source text. Supported by quotes: 'integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies', 'DT provides the ability to run simulations that reproduce and predict the behaviour', 'monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally'.

The extraction correctly identifies the Raspberry Pi as the embedded controller and the open-source GitHub repository as the source of hardware and software specifications. It accurately notes the use of calibration and monitoring algorithms and correctly assesses that no specific simulation engine or database is named (the text lists generic models and a communication server). Quotes: 'A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B... was selected to implement the controller', 'The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software specifications', 'RabbitMQ server was deployed for synchronisation and communication'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The characteristic description states 'Not in Document'. The source documents do not contain specific information regarding synchronization frequencies between physical and digital components, time scales for different services, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs for the Digital Twin. The text mentions a 'bang-bang' controller that waits after actuation and a 'periodic' read of temperature data, but does not define the Digital Twin's temporal specifications or synchronization scales.

The characteristic description states 'Not in Document'. The source documents describe a single incubator system with a single Raspberry Pi controller and a corresponding Digital Twin framework. There is no mention of multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures concerning multiple twins, or coordination mechanisms between different twin instances. The text focuses on a specific case study of one system.

The description accurately synthesizes the orchestration described in the source text. The text states: 'DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies'. It details the flow: 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment... resulting data is then used to inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally'. It also mentions: 'humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical twin'. Figure 1 is referenced as 'An example common structure of a DT'.

The characteristic description states 'Not in Document'. The source documents discuss the internal integration of the incubator system (e.g., Raspberry Pi, RabbitMQ, sensors, actuators) and the general framework of a DT. While RabbitMQ is mentioned for communication between the controller and the DT components, there is no mention of integration with external systems, enterprise systems, other digital twins, or specific interoperability standards outside of the local system setup.

1

not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).

Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the details of the two simulation models, their parameter counts, their analytical nature (differential equations), and the calibration process using collected data to minimize error. Supported by quotes: 'We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters.', 'Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia of the heatbed itself', 'A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters... minimizes the residual error.'

The description correctly identifies the resistive heatbed as the actuator and the Raspberry Pi as the controller, with a bang-bang control strategy. It mentions communication via RabbitMQ and Wi-Fi/Bluetooth, which aligns with the source documents. However, it incorrectly states 'fan' as part of the actuation hardware (the text describes the fan as part of the plant but not as an actuator controlled by the bang-bang controller). The extraction also correctly notes safety protocols in the low-level driver. The extraction is specific but slightly inaccurate regarding the fan's role in actuation. Key quotes: 'The heatbed was used as the surface heat source.', 'The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller.', 'In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is incorporated into the low-level driver.'

The extraction accurately describes the DS18S20 sensors, their quantity (3), placement (2 inside, 1 outside), measurement range (-55°C to +125°C), accuracy (±0.5°C), and communication via 1-Wire bus to the Raspberry Pi. It correctly notes that exact sampling frequency is not specified in the documents. This is highly detailed and well-supported by the text, particularly Section 2.1.5. Key quotes: 'DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer was used for measuring the temperature.', 'This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C.', 'Three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature.'

The extraction provides a good summary of the experimental validation, the comparison between the two models, and the use of calibration to reduce error. It correctly identifies the 4-parameter model as having higher fidelity regarding thermal inertia. The mention of 'sensor noise' as an uncertainty source is slightly speculative but consistent with the context of measurement discrepancies. Supported by quotes: 'Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia of the heatbed itself', 'The model (eq. (9)) considered more detailed than Model A, has four parameters', 'minimizes the residual error'.

PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of

block_5_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.

represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.

Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.
